news_summary: Ancient exercises, effective medications, practical fitness tests, and achievable daily step goals are all proving powerful in promoting longevity, managing weight, and improving heart health.
news_summary_fr: Les exercices traditionnels, les médicaments efficaces, les tests de condition physique pratiques et les objectifs quotidiens de pas réalisables s'avèrent tous très efficaces pour favoriser la longévité, contrôler son poids et améliorer la santé cardiaque.
news_summary_es: Los ejercicios tradicionales, los medicamentos eficaces, las pruebas físicas prácticas y los objetivos diarios de pasos realistas están demostrando ser muy eficaces para promover la longevidad, controlar el peso y mejorar la salud cardíaca.

articles.title: This 800-year-old Chinese exercise helps lower blood pressure naturally
articles.summary: An ancient Chinese exercise routine may be just as powerful as a daily brisk walk for lowering blood pressure — without equipment, gyms, or intense workouts. In a major clinical trial, adults with stage 1 hypertension who practiced baduanjin, a gentle mind-body exercise combining slow movements, breathing, and meditation, saw meaningful drops in blood pressure within three months that lasted for an entire year.
articles.content:
An ancient Chinese exercise practice that combines slow movements, controlled breathing, and meditation may help lower blood pressure as effectively as brisk walking, according to a large randomized clinical trial published in JACC, the flagship journal of the American College of Cardiology. Participants experienced improvements within three months, and those benefits continued for a full year.

High blood pressure remains one of the biggest preventable contributors to heart disease. Doctors often recommend regular physical activity to help manage it, but many people struggle to maintain long-term exercise habits, especially when workouts require gym memberships, special equipment, dedicated spaces, or ongoing coaching.

The practice studied, called baduanjin, is a traditional Chinese exercise made up of eight structured movements that combine aerobic activity, flexibility training, isometric exercise, and mindfulness. It has been practiced for centuries in China and is commonly performed in parks and community settings.

A typical session lasts about 10-15 minutes and does not require equipment or extensive training, making it easy to do in many different environments. Because the exercises are considered low- to moderate-intensity, researchers say the routine is accessible and safe for many adults.

"Given its simplicity, safety and ease at which one can maintain long-term adherence, baduanjin can be implemented as an effective, accessible and scalable lifestyle intervention for individuals trying to reduce their blood pressure," said Jing Li, MD, PhD, senior author of the study and Director, Department of Preventive Medicine, National Center for Cardiovascular Diseases in Beijing, China.

Clinical Trial Shows Lasting Blood Pressure Reductions

Researchers conducted the first large multicenter randomized clinical trial focused on how baduanjin affects blood pressure. The study followed 216 adults across seven communities and tracked changes in 24-hour systolic blood pressure at 12 weeks and again at 52 weeks.

Participants were at least 40 years old and had systolic blood pressure readings between 130-139 mm Hg, a range classified as stage 1 hypertension under ACC/AHA guidelines. They were divided into three groups for the yearlong intervention: baduanjin practice, self-directed exercise, or brisk walking.

Compared with self-directed exercise alone, people practicing baduanjin five days a week lowered their 24-hour systolic blood pressure by about 3 mg Hg and reduced office systolic blood pressure by 5 mg Hg after both three months and one year. Researchers noted that these reductions are similar to results seen with some first-line blood pressure medications.

The study also found that baduanjin produced results and safety outcomes comparable to brisk walking after one year.

Simple Routine Could Be Easy To Maintain

One of the most notable findings was that participants maintained the benefits even without continued monitoring or supervision. Maintaining long-term participation is often one of the biggest obstacles for lifestyle-based health interventions.

"Baduanjin has been practiced in China for over 800 years, and this study demonstrates how ancient, accessible, low-cost approaches can be validated through high-quality randomized research," said Harlan M. Krumholz, MD, FACC, Editor-in-Chief of JACC and the Harold H. Hines, Jr Professor at the Yale School of Medicine. "The blood pressure effect size is similar to that seen in landmark drug trials, but achieved without medication, cost or side effects. This makes it highly scalable for community-based prevention, including in resource-limited settings."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large, rigorous clinical trial demonstrating that an ancient, accessible exercise can significantly lower blood pressure, a major health risk factor. The intervention is low-cost, safe, easy to maintain, and has comparable effects to medication and brisk walking, offering broad public health benefits and scalable prevention especially in resource-limited settings.
articles.personality_title: Ancient Chinese exercise lowers blood pressure as well as brisk walking
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – High blood pressure is a common health problem that increases the risk of heart disease. Doctors often suggest exercise to help control it, but many people find it hard to keep up with workouts that need gyms or special equipment.

**What happened** – A new study tested baduanjin, an 800-year-old Chinese exercise that mixes slow movements, breathing, and meditation. In the study, 216 adults with mild high blood pressure practiced baduanjin for 10-15 minutes, five days a week, for a year. Their blood pressure dropped as much as it would with brisk walking or some medicines.

**Impact** – This exercise is simple, safe, and does not need any equipment or special places. People kept doing it and kept their blood pressure lower even without help from doctors. This means baduanjin can be an easy and low-cost way for many people to reduce blood pressure, especially where gyms or medicines are hard to get.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope more people will try baduanjin and that health programs might include it as a tool to help control blood pressure. Future studies may explore how it helps other health problems and how to teach it widely.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Practicing baduanjin, a gentle ancient Chinese exercise, can lower blood pressure as effectively as brisk walking or some medicines, making it an easy and affordable option for many adults.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un exercice chinois ancien réduit la tension artérielle aussi bien que la marche rapide
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'hypertension artérielle est un problème de santé courant qui augmente le risque de maladies cardiaques. Les médecins recommandent souvent de faire de l'exercice, mais beaucoup ont du mal à maintenir une activité physique régulière nécessitant une salle de sport ou du matériel spécial.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude a testé le baduanjin, un exercice chinois vieux de 800 ans qui combine mouvements lents, respiration contrôlée et méditation. Dans l'étude, 216 adultes avec une hypertension légère ont pratiqué le baduanjin 10-15 minutes, cinq jours par semaine, pendant un an. Leur tension artérielle a baissé autant qu'avec la marche rapide ou certains médicaments.

**Impact** – Cet exercice est simple, sûr, et ne demande ni équipement ni lieu spécial. Les participants ont continué à le faire et ont conservé une tension plus basse même sans suivi médical. Cela montre que le baduanjin peut être une méthode facile et peu coûteuse pour réduire la tension, surtout là où l'accès aux salles de sport ou aux médicaments est limité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que plus de personnes essaieront le baduanjin et que les programmes de santé l'intègreront pour aider à contrôler la tension artérielle. De futures études pourraient explorer ses effets sur d'autres problèmes de santé et comment le diffuser largement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pratiquer le baduanjin, un exercice chinois ancien et doux, peut réduire la tension artérielle aussi efficacement que la marche rapide ou certains médicaments, offrant une option facile et abordable pour de nombreux adultes.
articles.personality_title_es: Ejercicio chino antiguo reduce la presión arterial tan bien como caminar rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La presión arterial alta es un problema de salud común que aumenta el riesgo de enfermedades del corazón. Los médicos suelen recomendar ejercicio, pero muchas personas encuentran difícil mantenerlo si requiere gimnasios o equipos especiales.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio evaluó el baduanjin, un ejercicio chino de 800 años que combina movimientos lentos, respiración y meditación. En el estudio, 216 adultos con hipertensión leve practicaron baduanjin 10-15 minutos, cinco días a la semana, durante un año. Su presión arterial bajó tanto como con caminar rápido o algunos medicamentos.

**Impacto** – Este ejercicio es simple, seguro y no necesita equipo ni lugares especiales. Las personas continuaron haciéndolo y mantuvieron su presión arterial baja sin supervisión médica. Esto significa que el baduanjin puede ser una forma fácil y económica para que muchas personas reduzcan su presión, especialmente donde no hay fácil acceso a gimnasios o medicinas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que más personas prueben baduanjin y que los programas de salud lo incluyan para ayudar a controlar la presión arterial. Estudios futuros podrían investigar cómo ayuda con otros problemas de salud y cómo enseñarlo ampliamente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Practicar baduanjin, un ejercicio suave y antiguo chino, puede bajar la presión arterial tan efectivamente como caminar rápido o algunos medicamentos, siendo una opción fácil y accesible para muchos adultos.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly colored painting of eight flowing, interconnected brushstroke patterns arranged in a circle, symbolizing the eight movements of baduanjin, set against a calm park-like background with gentle hills and a rising sun, evoking balance, mindfulness, and natural health without any human figures.

articles.title: Ozempic delivers major weight loss in adults over 65, study finds
articles.summary: A major new analysis suggests semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) works remarkably well in adults over 65, helping many lose substantial amounts of weight while improving heart and metabolic health. Participants taking the drug lost over 15% of their body weight on average — far more than those receiving placebo treatment. Many also moved out of obesity categories entirely and reached healthier weight levels.
articles.content:
A new analysis of the STEP trials suggests that semaglutide, the active ingredient in the popular weight loss and diabetes drugs Wegovy and Ozempic, remains both effective and generally safe for adults over age 65 with obesity. Researchers found that older adults taking the once weekly obesity medication experienced substantial weight loss and improvements in several important health measures, with results similar to those seen in the broader STEP clinical trial population.

The study was led by Prof Luca Busetto of the University of Padova in Italy along with colleagues, including researchers from Novo Nordisk, the manufacturer of semaglutide and sponsor of the study.

Semaglutide Examined in Older Adults

Older adults with obesity are often considered medically vulnerable because they frequently have multiple health conditions and greater frailty, which can increase the risk of adverse events (AEs). Researchers say there is still limited information about how glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists such as semaglutide perform in this age group.

To better understand the drug's effects, investigators analyzed data from the STEP 1, 3, 4, 5, 8 and 9 trials. The analysis only included people with obesity or overweight who did not have diabetes because weight loss results in obesity drug trials are typically lower in people with diabetes, making direct comparisons difficult.

Participants were aged ≥65 years and had a body mass index of at least 30 kg/m², or at least 27 kg/m² with at least 1 obesity-related complication (without diabetes). They were randomly assigned to receive either semaglutide 2.4 mg or a placebo. All participants also received lifestyle intervention, while those in STEP 3 additionally received intensive behavioral therapy.

Researchers evaluated outcomes over 68 weeks. These included changes in body weight, waist circumference, waist to height ratio (WHtR), BMI category, and cardiometabolic risk factors such as blood pressure, cholesterol, blood fats, glucose measures and hs-CRP. Adverse events (AEs) were also monitored.

Significant Weight Loss and Health Improvements

Among the total trial population (N=4523), 358 participants were aged 65 or older and included in the analysis. Of those, 248 received semaglutide 2.4 mg and 110 received placebo. Most participants (90%) were between ages 65-74 years, while the remainder were 75 years and older.

At the beginning of the study, the average participant age was 69 years. Average body weight was 99.0 kg, average BMI was 36.6 kg/m², and average waist circumference was 115 cm. Women accounted for 72% of participants.

By week 68, participants receiving semaglutide experienced an average body weight reduction of −15.4%, compared with −5.1% in the placebo group. Waist circumference also dropped more sharply with semaglutide, decreasing by an average of −14.3 cm versus −6.0 cm with placebo.

The percentage of participants achieving major weight loss milestones was also much higher in the semaglutide group. Weight reductions of at least 10% were achieved by 66.5% of semaglutide participants compared with 15.5% receiving placebo. For at least 15% weight loss, the figures were 46.8% versus 6.4%, while 28.6% of semaglutide users lost at least 20% of their body weight compared with 2.7% in the placebo group.

Improvements in BMI and Cardiometabolic Health

Researchers also found notable improvements in waist to height ratio and BMI categories among semaglutide users.

In the semaglutide group, 11.3% achieved a WHtR <0.53, compared with 4.5% in the placebo group. More participants receiving semaglutide also moved into healthier BMI categories over the course of the study (see Figure full abstract).

A BMI of <27 kg/m² (so called healthy weight) was achieved by 27.0% of participants taking semaglutide, compared with 5.5% of those receiving placebo. The proportions of participants classified as overweight or in obesity class I, II and III all declined in the semaglutide group as more individuals reached healthier weight ranges.

For participants achieving both a BMI of 27 or less and a WHtR of <0.53 these values were 10.5% vs 2.7%, respectively.

The semaglutide group also showed greater improvements in cardiometabolic risk factors (see Table full abstract), including blood pressure, cholesterol, blood fats and glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c - a measure of blood sugar control used in diagnosis of diabetes).

Safety Findings and Side Effects

Overall rates of adverse events (AEs) were similar between groups, affecting 89.1% of semaglutide participants and 84.5% of placebo participants. However, serious adverse events were reported more frequently in the semaglutide group, occurring in 19.0% of participants compared with 12.7% in the placebo group.

Constipation and dizziness, which are known side effects associated with this type of medication, were more common with semaglutide. Rates of fractures and hypoglycemia were similar between groups and affected less than 1% of participants in each group.

Dr. Busetto concludes: "In this analysis of individuals with obesity aged 65 years and older, semaglutide reduced body weight and improved cardiometabolic risk factors compared with placebo, and the safety and efficacy profile of semaglutide was consistent with that reported in the STEP program."

He adds: "In many countries, including many high-income countries, the majority of the cases of excess weight actually occur in adults aged 65 years and over, representing a major driver for obesity-related complications and an important cause of reduced quality of life and disability. Our results support the use of semaglutide in this patient group."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough showing that semaglutide (Ozempic) effectively induces substantial weight loss and improves cardiometabolic health in adults over 65, a medically vulnerable population. The study is based on rigorous clinical trial data with meaningful health benefits that can improve quality of life and reduce obesity-related complications in a large demographic group. The article provides detailed context on the study design, outcomes, and safety, making it a substantive and impactful health advancement.
articles.personality_title: Study shows Ozempic helps adults over 65 lose significant weight and improve health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many adults over 65 struggle with obesity, which can cause other health problems. Doctors have been unsure if the drug semaglutide, known as Ozempic or Wegovy, works well and is safe for this older group.

**What happened** – Researchers studied data from six clinical trials involving 358 adults aged 65 and older with obesity but no diabetes. They took either semaglutide or a placebo while also following lifestyle advice. After 68 weeks, those on semaglutide lost an average of 15.4% of their body weight, compared to 5.1% in the placebo group. Many also improved their body measurements and heart-related health factors.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows semaglutide can safely help older adults lose a lot of weight and reduce risks like high blood pressure and cholesterol. Nearly half of the people on the drug lost at least 15% of their weight, and many reached healthier weight categories, which can improve quality of life.

**What's next step** – Doctors may start recommending semaglutide more often for older adults with obesity. Further studies might explore how to best use the drug alongside other treatments to keep people healthy longer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Semaglutide helps adults over 65 lose significant weight and improve heart and metabolic health safely over more than a year of treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude montre que Ozempic aide les plus de 65 ans à perdre beaucoup de poids et à améliorer leur santé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux adultes de plus de 65 ans souffrent d'obésité, ce qui peut causer d'autres problèmes de santé. Les médecins n'étaient pas sûrs que le médicament sémaglutide, appelé Ozempic ou Wegovy, soit efficace et sûr pour ce groupe plus âgé.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié les données de six essais cliniques impliquant 358 adultes âgés de 65 ans et plus en surpoids, sans diabète. Ils ont pris soit du sémaglutide, soit un placebo, tout en suivant des conseils sur le mode de vie. Après 68 semaines, ceux sous sémaglutide ont perdu en moyenne 15,4 % de leur poids, contre 5,1 % dans le groupe placebo. Beaucoup ont aussi amélioré leurs mesures corporelles et leur santé cardiaque.

**Impact** – Cela montre que le sémaglutide peut aider en toute sécurité les personnes âgées à perdre beaucoup de poids et à réduire des risques comme l'hypertension et le cholestérol. Près de la moitié des participants sous médicament ont perdu au moins 15 % de leur poids, atteignant des catégories de poids plus saines, ce qui peut améliorer la qualité de vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les médecins pourraient recommander plus souvent le sémaglutide aux personnes âgées obèses. D'autres études exploreront comment mieux utiliser ce médicament avec d'autres traitements pour garder les patients en bonne santé plus longtemps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le sémaglutide aide les plus de 65 ans à perdre beaucoup de poids et à améliorer leur santé cardiaque et métabolique sur plus d'un an de traitement, en toute sécurité.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio muestra que Ozempic ayuda a mayores de 65 años a perder peso y mejorar su salud
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas mayores de 65 años luchan con la obesidad, lo que puede causar otros problemas de salud. Los médicos no estaban seguros de si el medicamento semaglutida, conocido como Ozempic o Wegovy, funciona bien y es seguro para este grupo mayor.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores analizaron datos de seis ensayos clínicos con 358 adultos de 65 años o más con obesidad pero sin diabetes. Tomaron semaglutida o un placebo y siguieron consejos de estilo de vida. Después de 68 semanas, los que usaron semaglutida perdieron en promedio 15.4% de su peso corporal, comparado con 5.1% en el grupo placebo. Muchos también mejoraron sus medidas corporales y factores de salud cardíaca.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la semaglutida puede ayudar de manera segura a los adultos mayores a perder mucho peso y disminuir riesgos como presión alta y colesterol. Casi la mitad perdió al menos 15% de peso y muchos alcanzaron categorías de peso más saludables, lo que puede mejorar su calidad de vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los médicos podrían recomendar más la semaglutida para adultos mayores con obesidad. Se podrían hacer más estudios para ver cómo usar mejor el medicamento junto con otros tratamientos para mantener la salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – La semaglutida ayuda a mayores de 65 años a perder peso importante y mejorar su salud cardíaca y metabólica de forma segura durante más de un año de tratamiento.
articles.image_prompt: An elegant, detailed painting of a sturdy, mature oak tree shedding heavy, oversized leaves symbolizing excess weight, with smaller, healthier leaves emerging to represent significant weight loss and improved health, set against a soft, natural background of gentle hills and a calm sky in warm, earthy tones.

articles.title: This simple strength test could predict how long you live
articles.summary: Staying strong may be one of the biggest secrets to living longer — especially for older women. A major study of more than 5,000 women found that simple signs of muscle strength, like a firm hand grip or the ability to quickly stand up from a chair, were strongly linked to lower risk of death over the next eight years.
articles.content:
Getting enough exercise is important for healthy aging, but new research suggests muscle strength may play an equally critical role. A large study led by researchers at the University at Buffalo found that older women with greater strength had a significantly lower risk of death, even after accounting for physical activity levels, cardiovascular fitness, and inflammation.

The findings, published in JAMA Network Open, were based on more than 5,000 women between the ages of 63 and 99. Researchers tracked participants for eight years and discovered that women with stronger grip strength and faster sit-to-stand chair test times were more likely to live longer.

Muscle Strength Linked to Lower Mortality

The study focused on two simple measures commonly used in medical settings to evaluate strength in older adults: grip strength and chair stands. For the chair stand test, participants completed five unassisted sit-to-stand movements as quickly as possible.

Women who performed better on these tests had notably lower mortality rates during the follow-up period. Researchers found that every additional 7 kilograms of grip strength was associated with an average 12% reduction in death risk. Faster chair stand performance was also linked to improved survival, with a 4% lower mortality rate for every 6-second improvement from the slowest to the fastest times.

Importantly, these results remained significant even after researchers adjusted for physical activity and sedentary behavior using accelerometer data, gait speed (an indicator of cardiovascular fitness), and levels of C-reactive protein, a blood marker of inflammation associated with muscle decline and premature death.

Why Strength Matters for Healthy Aging

"If you don't have enough muscle strength to get up, it is going to be hard to do aerobic activities, such as walking, which is the most commonly reported recreational activity in U.S. adults ages 65 and older," says study lead author Michael LaMonte, PhD, research professor of epidemiology and environmental health in UB's School of Public Health and Health Professions.

"Muscular strength, in many ways, enables one to move their body from one point to another, particularly when moving against gravity," LaMonte adds. "Healthy aging probably is best pursued through adequate amounts of both aerobic and muscle-strengthening physical activities. When we no longer can get out of the chair and move around, we are in trouble."

According to LaMonte, this is the largest study so far to examine how muscle strength relates to longevity in women over 60. Earlier large-scale studies often lacked detailed measures of physical activity, cardiovascular fitness, and inflammation, making it harder to isolate the role of muscular strength alone.

Researchers also found that body size did not explain the relationship between strength and longevity.

"We also showed that differences in body size did not explain the muscular strength relationship with death," he says. "When we scaled the strength measures to body weight and even to lean body mass, there remained significantly lower mortality."

Strength Benefits Extended Beyond Exercise Guidelines

One of the study's most notable findings was that women who did not meet current physical activity recommendations still benefited from greater muscle strength. Current guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes of moderate intensity aerobic activity each week.

Even among women below that threshold, higher strength levels were associated with significantly lower mortality risk. LaMonte says this provides strong evidence that muscle-strengthening activities deserve greater emphasis in public health recommendations, particularly for older adults.

"Because women ages 80 and older are the fastest growing U.S. age group, the importance of monitoring and maintaining muscular strength will have huge public health implications in the coming decades," he says.

Simple Ways Older Adults Can Build Strength

Muscle-building activities do not necessarily require expensive gym equipment or intense workouts. Researchers note that free weights, dumbbells, weight machines, and bodyweight exercises such as modified push-ups, wall presses, and knee bends can all help improve strength.

LaMonte says everyday household items may also work as resistance tools.

"Even using soup cans or books as a form of resistance provides stimulus to skeletal muscles and could be used by individuals for whom other options are not feasible."

He advises older adults to speak with a health care provider before beginning a muscle-strengthening program. Those unfamiliar with strength training may also benefit from guidance from a physical therapist or exercise specialist to help ensure safety and appropriate progress.

The study also included researchers from the National Cancer Institute, University of California San Diego, Texas A&M University, Brown University, Stanford University, and Fred Hutch Cancer Center.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large, well-conducted study demonstrating that simple measures of muscle strength in older women strongly predict longevity, independent of other health factors. This finding has broad public health significance given the aging population and provides actionable insights for improving healthy aging through accessible muscle-strengthening activities. The study's scale, detailed analysis, and practical recommendations offer substantial, inspiring benefits for a large demographic.
articles.personality_title: Simple strength tests in older women predict longer life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A study has looked at how muscle strength affects the chances of living longer, especially in older women. While exercise is known to help aging, this research focused on muscle strength as a key factor.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University at Buffalo studied over 5,000 women aged 63 to 99 for eight years. They measured muscle strength using two easy tests: how strong a woman’s hand grip was and how fast she could stand up from a chair five times. Women with stronger grips and faster chair stands were less likely to die during the study period.

**Impact** – The study showed that every extra 7 kilograms of grip strength lowered the risk of death by 12%, and faster chair stands also improved survival chances. This link stayed strong even when considering exercise levels, heart health, and inflammation. Importantly, women who didn’t meet usual exercise guidelines still benefited from being stronger. This means muscle strength alone is a powerful sign of better health and longer life.

**What’s next step** – Experts suggest older adults should focus more on building muscle strength through simple activities like using light weights, bodyweight exercises, or even household items like soup cans. Doctors and therapists can help create safe plans. Future health advice may put more attention on strength training for older people to help them stay healthier longer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Simple tests of muscle strength in older women can predict longer life, showing that building strength is important for healthy aging.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des tests simples de force chez les femmes âgées prédisent une vie plus longue
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une étude a examiné comment la force musculaire influence les chances de vivre plus longtemps, surtout chez les femmes âgées. Bien que l’exercice soit reconnu pour aider à bien vieillir, cette recherche a mis l’accent sur la force musculaire comme facteur clé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Buffalo ont suivi plus de 5 000 femmes âgées de 63 à 99 ans pendant huit ans. Ils ont mesuré la force musculaire avec deux tests simples : la force de la poignée de main et la rapidité pour se lever d’une chaise cinq fois. Les femmes avec une meilleure force et une performance plus rapide avaient moins de risques de mourir pendant l’étude.

**Impact** – L’étude a montré que chaque 7 kilogrammes supplémentaires de force de poignée réduisaient le risque de décès de 12 %, et une meilleure performance au test de chaise améliorait aussi les chances de survie. Ce lien est resté fort même en tenant compte du niveau d’activité physique, de la santé cardiaque et de l’inflammation. Fait important, même les femmes ne respectant pas les recommandations d’exercice bénéficiaient d’une force accrue. Cela montre que la force musculaire seule est un signe puissant de meilleure santé et longévité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts recommandent aux personnes âgées de se concentrer davantage sur le renforcement musculaire avec des activités simples comme utiliser des poids légers, des exercices au poids du corps, ou même des objets ménagers comme des boîtes de conserve. Les médecins et les thérapeutes peuvent aider à créer des plans sûrs. Les futures recommandations de santé pourraient insister davantage sur l’entraînement de la force pour aider les personnes âgées à rester en meilleure santé plus longtemps.

**Phrase clé** – Des tests simples de force musculaire chez les femmes âgées peuvent prédire une vie plus longue, montrant que renforcer ses muscles est important pour bien vieillir.
articles.personality_title_es: Pruebas simples de fuerza en mujeres mayores predicen una vida más larga
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un estudio investigó cómo la fuerza muscular afecta las probabilidades de vivir más tiempo, especialmente en mujeres mayores. Aunque se sabe que el ejercicio ayuda a envejecer bien, esta investigación se centró en la fuerza muscular como un factor importante.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Buffalo estudiaron a más de 5,000 mujeres de entre 63 y 99 años durante ocho años. Midieron la fuerza muscular con dos pruebas simples: qué tan fuerte era el agarre de la mano y qué tan rápido podían levantarse de una silla cinco veces. Las mujeres con un agarre más fuerte y tiempos más rápidos tenían menos probabilidades de morir durante el estudio.

**Impacto** – El estudio mostró que cada 7 kilogramos adicionales de fuerza en el agarre reducía el riesgo de muerte en un 12 %, y un mejor rendimiento en la prueba de levantarse de la silla también mejoraba las posibilidades de supervivencia. Esta relación se mantuvo incluso al considerar los niveles de actividad física, la salud del corazón y la inflamación. Lo importante es que incluso las mujeres que no cumplían con las recomendaciones de ejercicio se beneficiaron por tener más fuerza. Esto indica que la fuerza muscular por sí sola es una señal poderosa de mejor salud y mayor longevidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos sugieren que los adultos mayores deben enfocarse más en desarrollar fuerza muscular mediante actividades simples como usar pesas ligeras, ejercicios con el propio peso corporal o incluso objetos del hogar como latas de sopa. Los médicos y terapeutas pueden ayudar a crear planes seguros. Las futuras recomendaciones de salud podrían poner más atención en el entrenamiento de fuerza para que los adultos mayores se mantengan saludables por más tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Pruebas simples de fuerza muscular en mujeres mayores pueden predecir una vida más larga, mostrando que fortalecer los músculos es importante para envejecer saludablemente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, weathered hand firmly gripping a simple wooden chair armrest beside a softly glowing hourglass, both resting on a gentle, earth-toned background symbolizing strength and the passage of time in healthy aging.

articles.title: Scientists say 8,500 steps a day could stop weight from creeping back
articles.summary: A new international analysis suggests there may be a surprisingly simple secret to keeping weight off after dieting: walking about 8,500 steps a day. Researchers found that people who boosted their daily steps to around that level during a weight-loss program — and kept it up afterward — were far more successful at avoiding the frustrating cycle of regaining lost weight. The study highlights a major challenge in obesity treatment, since most people regain much of the weight they lose within a few years.
articles.content:
New research to be presented at this year's European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (May 12-15) suggests that walking around 8,500 steps a day may help people avoid regaining weight after dieting. The findings will also be published in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health.

Many weight loss programs encourage people to walk more each day, but researchers say there has been limited evidence showing whether increasing daily steps actually helps people lose weight while dieting. It has also remained unclear whether walking more can help people maintain weight loss over time and, if so, what step count may be most effective.

Why Preventing Weight Regain Matters

"The most important -- and greatest -- challenge when treating obesity is preventing weight regain," explains Professor Marwan El Ghoch, of the Department of Biomedical, Metabolic and Neural Sciences, University of Modena and Reggio Emilia, Modena, Italy.

"Around 80% of people with overweight or obesity who initially lose weight tend to put some or all of it back on again within three to five years.

"The identification of a strategy that would solve this problem and help people maintain their new weight would be of huge clinical value."

To investigate further, Professor El Ghoch and colleagues from Italy and Lebanon carried out a systematic review and meta-analysis of previous studies focused on walking and weight management.

Analysis Included Nearly 4,000 Adults

The researchers reviewed 18 randomized controlled trials. Of those, 14 studies involving 3,758 adults were included in the final meta-analysis. Participants had an average age of 53 years and an average BMI of 31 kg/m2. The studies included people from several countries, including the UK, US, Australia, and Japan.

The trials compared 1,987 participants enrolled in lifestyle modification (LSM) programs with 1,771 people in control groups who were either dieting without additional support or receiving no treatment.

The lifestyle modification programs combined dietary guidance with recommendations to walk more and track daily steps. These programs included an initial weight loss phase followed by a maintenance phase designed to help participants keep the weight off long term.

Researchers measured participants' daily step counts at the beginning of the studies, after the weight loss phase (average duration of 7.9 months), and after the maintenance phase (average duration of 10.3 months).

At the start of the trials, both groups had similar activity levels. Participants in the LSM group averaged 7,280 steps per day, while those in the control group averaged 7,180 steps daily.

Higher Step Counts Linked to Less Weight Regain

The control group did not significantly increase daily walking and did not experience weight loss during the studies.

Participants in the LSM programs, however, increased their daily step count to an average of 8,454 steps by the end of the weight loss phase. During that time, they lost an average of 4.39% of their body weight, equal to roughly 4 kg.

Participants largely maintained their higher activity levels throughout the maintenance phase, averaging 8,241 steps per day by the end of the studies. They also kept off most of the weight they had lost, with an average long-term weight loss of 3.28%, or about 3 kg.

Further analysis revealed a clear connection between higher daily step counts and reduced weight regain. Researchers found that people who increased their steps during the weight loss phase and continued that level of activity afterward were more successful at maintaining their weight loss.

Interestingly, walking more was not linked to greater weight loss during the initial dieting phase. Researchers believe this may be because factors such as calorie reduction have a stronger effect on short-term weight loss.

A Simple and Affordable Strategy

Professor El Ghosh says the findings show that lifestyle modification programs can support meaningful long-term weight loss.

He adds: "Participants should be always encouraged to increase their step count to approximately 8,500 a day during the weight loss phase and sustain this level of physical activity during the maintenance phase to help prevent them from regaining weight. "Increasing the number of steps walked to 8,500 each day is a simple and affordable strategy to prevent weight regain."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific finding that walking about 8,500 steps daily can help people maintain weight loss after dieting, addressing a major challenge in obesity treatment. The study is based on a large meta-analysis involving nearly 4,000 adults from multiple countries, providing substantial evidence and practical guidance with broad public health implications. The strategy is simple, affordable, and has potential to improve long-term health outcomes for many people worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Walking 8,500 steps daily helps keep weight off after dieting
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people struggle to keep weight off after losing it through dieting. Most regain the weight within a few years, making long-term weight loss a big challenge.

**What happened** – Scientists studied nearly 4,000 adults from several countries who took part in programs combining diet and walking. They found that people who walked about 8,500 steps a day during and after their weight loss kept the weight off better than those who did not increase their walking.

**Impact** – This research shows a clear link between walking regularly and preventing weight regain. Walking 8,500 steps a day is a simple, low-cost way to help people maintain weight loss, which can improve health and reduce problems related to obesity.

**What's next step** – Researchers suggest that weight loss programs should encourage people to reach and maintain about 8,500 steps daily. Future studies might explore how to help people keep this habit and if it works for different groups of people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Walking around 8,500 steps a day during and after dieting helps people maintain their weight loss and avoid regaining pounds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Marcher 8 500 pas par jour aide à garder son poids après un régime
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes ont du mal à garder leur poids après avoir perdu du poids grâce à un régime. La plupart reprennent le poids perdu en quelques années, ce qui rend la perte de poids durable difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié près de 4 000 adultes de plusieurs pays qui ont suivi des programmes combinant régime alimentaire et marche. Ils ont découvert que ceux qui marchaient environ 8 500 pas par jour pendant et après leur perte de poids réussissaient mieux à garder leur poids.

**Impact** – Cette recherche montre un lien clair entre la marche régulière et la prévention de la reprise de poids. Marcher 8 500 pas par jour est une méthode simple et peu coûteuse pour aider à maintenir la perte de poids, ce qui peut améliorer la santé et réduire les problèmes liés à l’obésité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs recommandent que les programmes de perte de poids encouragent les participants à atteindre et maintenir environ 8 500 pas par jour. Des études futures pourraient chercher comment aider les gens à garder cette habitude et vérifier si cela fonctionne pour différents groupes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Marcher environ 8 500 pas par jour pendant et après un régime aide à maintenir la perte de poids et à éviter de reprendre des kilos.
articles.personality_title_es: Caminar 8,500 pasos diarios ayuda a mantener el peso tras una dieta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas tienen dificultad para mantener el peso después de adelgazar con una dieta. La mayoría recupera el peso perdido en pocos años, lo que hace que perder peso a largo plazo sea un gran reto.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron a casi 4,000 adultos de varios países que participaron en programas que combinaban dieta y caminata. Descubrieron que quienes caminaban cerca de 8,500 pasos diarios durante y después de perder peso lograban mantenerlo mejor que quienes no aumentaban su actividad.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra una relación clara entre caminar regularmente y evitar recuperar peso. Caminar 8,500 pasos diarios es una forma sencilla y económica de ayudar a mantener la pérdida de peso, lo que puede mejorar la salud y reducir problemas relacionados con la obesidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que los programas para perder peso animen a las personas a alcanzar y mantener unos 8,500 pasos diarios. Estudios futuros podrían investigar cómo ayudar a mantener este hábito y si funciona en diferentes grupos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Caminar unos 8,500 pasos al día durante y después de una dieta ayuda a mantener la pérdida de peso y a evitar recuperar kilos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently winding path marked with subtle footprints leading steadily forward, beside a balanced scale symbolizing weight maintenance, set in a softly lit natural landscape with muted earth tones and simple greenery, conveying steady progress and healthy lifestyle without showing any people.
